result.markdown:
--- title: "Interactive KPI Dashboard" date: "2025-01-20" categories: [Python, Plotly, Dashboard, Visualisation] description: "A multi-panel business intelligence dashboard built with Plotly Express to track revenue, customer acquisition cost, and churn over time." page-layout: full toc: true code-fold: true --- ```{=html}
Plotly Python Dashboard BI
``` ## Overview A dashboard's job is simple in theory: put the right number in front of the right person at the right time. In practice it's trickier — you have to decide what actually matters, strip out everything that doesn't, and then make it readable at a glance. This project builds a multi-panel KPI dashboard for a fictional subscription business, tracking three core metrics over a 12-month period: monthly recurring revenue, customer acquisition cost, and churn rate. All charts are built with Plotly Express and styled consistently. --- ## Setup & Data ::: {#755cbfab .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import plotly.express as px import plotly.graph_objects as go from plotly.subplots import make_subplots np.random.seed(7) months = pd.period_range("2024-01", periods=12, freq="M") month_labels = [str(m) for m in months] mrr = np.cumsum(np.random.normal(3000, 600, 12)) + 40000 cac = 120 + np.random.normal(0, 10, 12) + np.linspace(0, -25, 12) churn = np.clip(0.065 + np.random.normal(0, 0.008, 12) + np.linspace(0, -0.02, 12), 0.02, 0.12) new_customers = (np.random.poisson(35, 12) + np.linspace(0, 20, 12)).astype(int) df = pd.DataFrame({ "month": month_labels, "mrr": mrr.round(0), "cac": cac.round(2), "churn_rate": (churn * 100).round(2), "new_customers": new_customers }) df.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
month mrr cac churn_rate new_customers
0 2024-01 44014.0 125.05 6.46 38
1 2024-02 46735.0 115.11 5.16 35
2 2024-03 49754.0 113.03 5.81 36
3 2024-04 52999.0 98.65 4.12 34
4 2024-05 55526.0 116.45 6.61 41
``` ::: ::: --- ## Monthly Recurring Revenue ::: {#f4680f46 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = px.area( df, x="month", y="mrr", title="Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR)", labels={"month": "", "mrr": "MRR (€)"}, color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e"] ) fig.update_traces(fill='tozeroy', fillcolor='rgba(127,255,110,0.08)', line_width=2.5) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535", tickangle=-30), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), ) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: MRR is growing steadily — the upward slope steepens slightly in Q3, which correlates with the increase in new customer acquisition visible later. --- ## Customer Acquisition Cost ::: {#67f48130 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = px.bar( df, x="month", y="cac", title="Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)", labels={"month": "", "cac": "CAC (€)"}, color="cac", color_continuous_scale=["#7fff6e", "#252535"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)", tickangle=-30), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), coloraxis_showscale=False ) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: CAC is trending down — a good sign that either marketing efficiency is improving or organic/referral channels are picking up share. --- ## Churn Rate Over Time ::: {#e4d5ef4f .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = px.line( df, x="month", y="churn_rate", title="Monthly Churn Rate (%)", labels={"month": "", "churn_rate": "Churn Rate (%)"}, markers=True, color_discrete_sequence=["#68d9ff"] ) fig.add_hline(y=5, line_dash="dash", line_color="#5a5a72", annotation_text="5% target", annotation_font_color="#5a5a72") fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535", tickangle=-30), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), ) fig.update_traces(line_width=2.5) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Churn opens above the 5% target in early months but trends down toward it by year-end. The trajectory is encouraging, though it hasn't yet broken through the threshold consistently. --- ## Combined KPI Overview All four metrics on one canvas for executive summary use. ::: {#5385ca11 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = make_subplots( rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=("MRR (€)", "CAC (€)", "Churn Rate (%)", "New Customers"), vertical_spacing=0.14, horizontal_spacing=0.10 ) # MRR fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=df["month"], y=df["mrr"], mode="lines", fill="tozeroy", fillcolor="rgba(127,255,110,0.07)", line=dict(color="#7fff6e", width=2)), row=1, col=1) # CAC fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=df["month"], y=df["cac"], marker_color="#a78bfa"), row=1, col=2) # Churn fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=df["month"], y=df["churn_rate"], mode="lines+markers", line=dict(color="#68d9ff", width=2)), row=2, col=1) # New customers fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=df["month"], y=df["new_customers"], marker_color="#ffd166"), row=2, col=2) fig.update_layout( showlegend=False, plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_text="Business KPI Summary — 2024", title_font_color="#ffffff", height=550 ) for i in fig.layout.annotations: i.font.color = "#e2e2e2" fig.update_xaxes(gridcolor="#252535", tickangle=-45, tickfont_size=9) fig.update_yaxes(gridcolor="#252535") fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## Takeaways :::{.callout-note} **Dashboard design decisions:** - Three metrics is usually better than ten. Every additional tile competes for attention — the discipline is in what you leave out. - The combined panel is useful for spotting correlations (CAC falling as new customers increase suggests referral effects, not just spend). - Plotly's hover interactivity adds real value for self-service exploration without any additional code complexity. ::: --- ## Reflection This project shifted my thinking about what "good" data visualisation means. My instinct was to make charts visually complex — gradient fills, annotations, reference lines everywhere. But the versions my classmates found most useful were the simpler ones with a clear single question per chart. The combined subplot at the end is the exception: it works because it's explicitly framed as a summary for someone who already knows what they're looking at. Context changes what's appropriate.

← Back to Projects
